AtomicFact.description: A single atomic fact extracted from a source.

direct_quote.description: Exact quote from source supporting this fact
direct_quote.title: Direct Quote

fact_category.description: Category: statistic, measurement, causal_link, named_entity, date_time, regulatory_threshold, standard_reference, geographic, methodology
fact_category.title: Fact Category

relevance_score.description: Relevance to the research question, 0.0 to 1.0
relevance_score.title: Relevance Score

confidence.description: Confidence that this fact is correctly extracted, 0.0 to 1.0
confidence.title: Confidence

perspective.description: STORM research perspective: Scientific, Regulatory, Industry, Economic, Public_Health, Historical, Regional, Methodological, Emerging_Trends
perspective.title: Perspective

entities.description: Key named entities (compounds, methods, organizations, metrics) mentioned in this fact. Used by the wiki mesh entity canonicalization pass. Optional; legacy data defaults to [].
entities.title: Entities

AtomicFact.title: AtomicFact

SourceAnalysis.description: Analysis of a single source document.

source_type.description: Type: journal_article, government_report, industry_report, news, standard, patent, book, other
source_type.title: Source Type

overall_relevance.description: Overall relevance to the research question, 0.0 to 1.0
overall_relevance.title: Overall Relevance

atomic_facts.description: All atomic facts extracted from this source
atomic_facts.title: Atomic Facts

evidence_summary.description: Brief summary of what this source contributes
evidence_summary.title: Evidence Summary

SourceAnalysis.title: SourceAnalysis

system:
You are a research evidence analyst. Extract atomic facts from source material.

Rules:
1. Extract 8-15 atomic facts per source. Focus on the MOST relevant, specific, and unique claims, statistics, findings, and conclusions. Quality over quantity — prioritize facts that DIRECTLY answer the research question over tangential details. Missing a highly relevant fact is worse than including marginally relevant ones.
   CRITICAL: ONLY extract from the ARTICLE BODY TEXT. NEVER extract from: navigation menus, cookie consent banners, URL paths/slugs, abbreviation/glossary tables, author byline metadata, page footers/headers, table of contents page numbers, product category labels, or marketing taglines. If the source is mostly non-content (nav, ads, cookie text), extract ZERO facts and set source_quality to 0.0.
2. Each atomic fact = ONE specific claim with the EXACT quote from the source (minimum 10 words, up to 200 chars). A quote shorter than 5 words is NEVER acceptable — skip that fact entirely.
3. Source quality: Score each source individually on 0.0-1.0 based on:
   - Peer review status (peer-reviewed journal > preprint > report > blog)
   - Author credentials (named experts > organizations > anonymous)
   - Recency (2023-2026 > 2018-2022 > older)
   - Specificity to research question (directly relevant > tangentially related)
   Do NOT use fixed scores for source types. Vary scores based on each source's specific qualities.
4. Rate relevance to the SPECIFIC research question on 0.0-1.0:
   - 0.9-1.0: Directly answers part of the research question with specific data
   - 0.7-0.8: Provides important context or supporting evidence
   - 0.5-0.6: Tangentially relevant or provides background
   - 0.1-0.4: Marginally relevant, mostly off-topic
   Each fact must get a UNIQUE relevance score. No two facts from the same source should have identical scores.
5. Include exact numbers, dates, measurements, names, standards.
6. For EVERY quantitative finding, extract ALL of the following when available:
   - Effect size with units (MD, SMD, WMD, OR, HR, RR)
   - 95% confidence interval (e.g., "95% CI -5.54 to -3.05")
   - Heterogeneity (I-squared value, e.g., "I²=96%")
   - Number of studies/trials included (e.g., "7 RCTs")
   - Total sample size (e.g., "n=269")
   - Evidence certainty/quality rating (e.g., "GRADE: moderate", "high certainty")
   - Study duration/follow-up period
   Missing any of these when the source provides them is a CRITICAL extraction failure.
7. For review/meta-analysis papers, extract findings from EACH cited study individually.
   Prioritize pooled estimates with I² and CI over narrative summaries.
8. For each atomic fact, tag the research perspective it belongs to.
9. CRITICAL (ARCH-4): The 'statement' field must be a DIRECT PARAPHRASE of the source text.
   Do NOT infer, extrapolate, or add information beyond what the direct_quote states.
   If the direct_quote is fewer than 10 words, the statement must be equally narrow in scope.
10. QUOTE LENGTH: The 'direct_quote' field MUST be 150 characters or fewer.
   Quote only the KEY phrase, not entire sentences or paragraphs.
   This forces you to identify the most important fragment of evidence.
   BAD: "A systematic review and meta-analysis of 24 randomized controlled trials totaling 1,768 participants found that alternate-day fasting produced weight loss comparable to continuous energy restriction"
   GOOD: "alternate-day fasting produced weight loss comparable to continuous energy restriction"
   A 2-word quote like 'distilled water' can only support a statement about distilled water,
   not a full sentence about healthcare settings or bacterial contamination.
   Perspectives: Scientific, Regulatory, Industry, Economic, Public_Health, Historical, Regional, Methodological, Emerging_Trends.
   Choose the single best-fit perspective.

Output format (return ONLY this JSON structure):
{"analyses": [{"source_url": "https://example.com", "source_title": "Study Title", "source_type": "journal_article", "source_quality": 0.8, "overall_relevance": 0.7, "year": 2024, "authors": ["Smith J"], "venue": "Water Research", "doi": "", "atomic_facts": [{"statement": "E. coli was detected in 30% of tested filters after 6 months", "direct_quote": "E. coli presence in 30% of tested POU filters", "fact_category": "statistic", "relevance_score": 0.9, "confidence": 0.8, "perspective": "Scientific"}], "evidence_summary": "Bacterial contamination in POU water filters"}]}
prompt:
Research question: What are the proven health benefits and risks of intermittent fasting in adults with metabolic syndrome?

Analyze the following 2 sources and extract the TOP 8-15 MOST
relevant atomic facts per source. Focus on the most specific, unique, and
directly relevant numbers, dates, measurements, claims, findings,
regulations, and standards. Quality over quantity.

Sources:
Source URL: http://www.thelancet.com/article/S2589537024000981/pdf
Source title: Intermittent fasting and health outcomes: an umbrella review of systematic reviews and meta-analyses of randomised controlled trials
Source type: academic
Content:
Summary Background Benefits of Intermittent fasting (IF) on health-related outcomes have been found in a range of randomised controlled trials (RCTs). Our umbrella review aimed to systematically analyze and synthesize the available causal evidence on IF and its impact on specific health-related outcomes while evaluating its evidence quality. Methods We comprehensively searched the PubMed, Embase, Web of Science, and Cochrane databases (from inception up to 8 January 2024) to identify related systematic reviews and meta-analyses of RCTs investigating the association between IF and human health outcomes. We recalculated the effect sizes for each meta-analysis as mean difference (MD) or standardized mean difference (SMD) with corresponding 95% confidence intervals (CIs). Subgroup analyses were performed for populations based on three specific status: diabetes, overweight or obesity, and metabolic syndrome. The quality of systematic reviews was evaluated using A Measurement Tool to Assess Systematic Reviews (AMSTAR), and the certainty of evidence was assessed using the Grading of Recommendations, Assessment, Development, and Evaluations (GRADE) system. This study is registered with PROSPERO (CRD42023382004). Findings A total of 351 associations from 23 meta-analyses with 34 health outcomes were included in the study. A wide range of outcomes were investigated, including anthropometric measures (n = 155), lipid profiles (n = 83), glycemic profiles (n = 57), circulatory system index (n = 41), appetite (n = 9), and others (n = 6). Twenty-one (91%) meta-analyses with 346 associations were rated as high confidence according to the AMSTAR criteria. The summary effects estimates were significant at p < 0.05 in 103 associations, of which 10 (10%) were supported by high certainty of evidence according to GRADE. Specifically, compared with non-intervention diet in adults with overweight or obesity, IF reduced waist circumference (WC) (MD = −1.02 cm; 95% CI: −1.99 to −0.06; p = 0.038), fat mass (MD = −0.72 kg; 95% CI: −1.32 to −0.12; p = 0.019), fasting insulin (SMD = −0.21; 95% CI: −0.40 to −0.02; p = 0.030), low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C) (SMD = −0.20; 95% CI: −0.38 to −0.02; p = 0.027), total cholesterol (TC) (SMD = −0.29; 95% CI: −0.48 to −0.10; p = 0.003), and triacylglycerols (TG) (SMD = −0.23; 95% CI: −0.39 to −0.06; p = 0.007), but increased fat free mass (FFM) (MD = 0.98 kg; 95% CI: 0.18–1.78; p = 0.016). Of note, compared with the non-intervention diet, modified alternate-day fasting (MADF) reduced fat mass (MD = −0.70 kg; 95% CI: −1.38 to −0.02; p = 0.044). In people with overweight or obesity, and type 2 diabetes, IF increases high-density lipoprotein cholesterol (HDL-C) levels compared to continuous energy restriction (CER) (MD = 0.03 mmol/L; 95% CI: 0.01–0.05; p = 0.010). However, IF was less effective at reducing systolic blood pressure (SBP) than a CER diet in adults with overweight or obesity (SMD = 0.21; 95% CI: 0.05–0.36; p = 0.008). Interpretation Our findings suggest that IF may have beneficial effects on a range of health outcomes for adults with overweight or obesity, compared to CER or non-intervention diet. Specifically, IF may decreased WC, fat mass, LDL-C, TG, TC, fasting insulin, and SBP, while increasing HDL-C and FFM. Notably, it is worth noting that the SBP lowering effect of IF appears to be weaker than that of CER. Funding This work was supported by the 10.13039/501100012166National Key Research and Development Program of China (Q-JW), the 10.13039/501100001809Natural Science Foundation of China (Q-JW and T-TG), Outstanding Scientific Fund of 10.13039/501100015226Shengjing Hospital of China Medical University (Q-JW), and 345 Talent Project of 10.13039/501100015226Shengjing Hospital of China Medical University (T-TG).
---Source URL: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC11009208
Source title: Intermittent fasting promotes rejuvenation of immunosenescent phenotypes in aged adipose tissue
Source type: academic
Content:
The aging of white adipose tissue (WAT) involves senescence of adipose stem and progenitor cells (ASPCs) and dysregulation of immune cell populations, serving as a major driver of age-associated adipose dysfunction and metabolic diseases. Conversely, the elimination of senescent ASPCs is associated with improvements in overall health. Intermittent fasting (IF), a dietary intervention that incorporates periodic cycles of fasting and refeeding, has been reported to promote weight loss and fat mass reduction and improve glucose and insulin homeostasis in both murine and human studies. While previous studies have assessed the effects of IF on obesity-associated metabolic dysfunction, few studies have examined the aging-specific changes to ASPCs and immune cell populations in WAT. Here, we show that IF in 18–20-month-old mice reduced senescent phenotypes of ASPCs and restored their adipogenic potential. Intriguingly, IF-treated mice exhibited an increase in adipose eosinophils, which has been reported to be associated with improved WAT homeostasis and immunological fitness in aged mice. The observed cellular and metabolic changes suggest that IF may be a feasible lifestyle regimen to reduce cellular senescence which could result in attenuation of downstream aging-induced WAT dysfunction and metabolic diseases.
The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1007/s11357-024-01093-4.
Keywords: Intermittent fasting, Immunosenescence, Visceral adipose, Adipose stem and progenitor cells, Eosinophils, Rejuvenation
Globally, the aging population has been accompanied by an increased prevalence of diseases such as type 2 diabetes, cancer, cardiovascular disease, neurodegenerative diseases, and arthritis [1]. Unhealthy white adipose tissue (WAT) expansion in obesity and its metabolic sequelae share similar cellular and molecular processes with those induced by aging and are associated with premature senescence [2, 3]. Aging results in accumulation and redistribution of adipose tissues in a depot-specific manner leading to excess deposition of visceral white adipose (WAT) depots, a progressive loss of subcutaneous fat, and a decline in brown and beige fat mass and activity, contributing to thermal dysregulation [4–6]. Visceral fat accumulation is associated with chronic inflammation, lipotoxicity in other organs, and metabolic diseases, including insulin resistance and cardiovascular disease, whereas subcutaneous fat has been reported to have a weaker correlation with insulin resistance [7, 8].
WAT is one of the earliest organs to display aging-induced changes, [9, 10] and it has been suggested that WAT-specific changes serve as early hallmarks and initiators of age-related chronic inflammatory diseases [11], driven by cellular senescence [3, 12]. The process of WAT aging results in substantial changes in the cellular composition of the stromal vascular fraction (SVF), which includes adipose stem and progenitor cells (ASPCs), immune cell populations, and vascular cells [13, 14]. Senescent ASPCs exhibit reduced self-renewal capacity and differentiation potential and increased expression of senescence markers such as p16Ink4a and p21, leading to hypertrophic adipose expansion and metabolic dysfunction of WAT [5]. Further, these cells secrete pro-inflammatory factors which impact the functionality of surrounding cells within adipose tissue [5, 15]. Previous studies have demonstrated that reducing cellular senescence in WAT alleviates systemic metabolic dysfunction [15–18]. Senescent ASPCs from human fat tissue inhibited adipogenesis in non-senescent progenitors in a paracrine manner, through secretion of several compounds including activin A [17]. Blocking activin A both in vivo and in vitro and selective clearance of senescent cells restored adipogenic potential in mice and improved glucose homeostasis and insulin sensitivity [17, 18]. The aging process also causes marked remodeling of WAT immune cell populations, leading to significant reduction in eosinophils and innate lymphoid cells (ILC2), which together play a critical role in maintenance of WAT homeostasis in young, lean, murine, and human populations [19, 20]. A recent study reported that adoptive transfer of visceral adipose eosinophils from young mice to aged recipients led to improved physical and immune fitness and reduced local and systemic inflammation [19]. Collectively, these data support that anti-aging strategies targeting senescent ASPCs and/or eosinophils can restore functional capacity and yield improvements in overall metabolic health in aged mouse models [21].
Caloric restriction (CR) has long been reported to promote overall health and longevity [22]. More recently, intermittent fasting (IF), a dietary intervention incorporating periodic cycles of food restriction, has gained popularity as an alternative to caloric restriction [23]. IF encompasses various regimens including every-other-day fasting, fasting-mimicking diets, and time-restricted feeding, where daily caloric intake occurs in a limited window of time [23]. Numerous studies have reported positive health outcomes associated with IF in both murine and clinical settings. This includes weight loss and reductions in fat mass, even in the absence of reduced energy intake [24, 25], improved glucose and insulin homeostasis, and rejuvenation of the immune system [26–28]. Most of these studies have focused on relatively young mouse models of diet-induced obesity or metabolically unhealthy overweight and obese adult populations [24, 25]. Few studies have examined the aging-specific changes to immune cell populations in adipose tissues at the proteomic level and the impact of IF on aging-induced cellular phenotypes in adipose tissues is largely unknown.
In this study, we examined the impact of IF on adipose cellular phenotypes in 18–20-month-old mice. Our findings reveal that a 10-week IF regimen in old mice led to rejuvenation of visceral fat ASPCs and an increase in eosinophils suggesting that IF may be a feasible lifestyle regimen to improve age-related cellular dysfunction.
Animals and diet intervention
All animal use protocols were approved by the Animal Care Committee of The Centre for Phenogenomics (TCP) according to the standards of the Canadian Council on Animal Care. Animals were kept in a specific pathogen-free (SPF) facility in ventilated cages under 12:12-h light:dark cycle at 22 ± 1 °C, with 30–60% humidity and unrestricted access to water and standard rodent chow diet (ND) (Harlan #2918; 17% fat). Aged 75-week-old body weight-matched male C57BL/6 J mice were randomly divided into ad libitum (AL) and intermittent fasting (IF) groups. IF intervention consisted of 2 days of free access to food followed by 24 h of fasting. Food intake and body weights were monitored throughout the course of the study. For comparison of immune cell populations between young and aged mice, we harvested WAT from 3-month-old male C57BL/6J mice.
Body composition and indirect calorimetry analysis
EchoMRI-100 body composition analyzer (Echo Medical Systems, TX, USA) was used to determine fat mass and lean mass at week 7 of the study. Energy expenditure was monitored through indirect calorimetry using Promethion Core systems (Sable Systems International, Las Vegas, USA) for 7 consecutive days. Animals were singly housed in Promethion control cabinets, with animal facility-matched cage environment. Using this system, different parameters such as oxygen (O2) consumption (mL/min), carbon dioxide (CO2) production (mL/min), respiratory exchange ratio (RER = VCO2/VO2), locomotor activity (counts/s), and food intake (g) were recorded. Measurements were taken at 15-min intervals. Energy expenditure (EE) was calculated and graphed using CalR (a web-based analysis tool for Indirect Calorimetry [29]). At sacrifice, tissues were weighed to the nearest 0.01 g for body composition analysis.
Glucose/insulin tolerance tests
Glucose and insulin tolerance tests (GTT and ITT) were performed at the end of the study. Briefly, mice were fasted for 16 h prior to intraperitoneal (IP) injection of glucose at a concentration of 1 mg/g of body weight for GTT. For ITT, animals were fasted for 6 h prior to IP injection of insulin (Humulin R, 0.75 mU/g of body weight). Circulating glucose levels were measured from the tail vein prior to injection (fasting glucose level) and at 15, 30, 60, and 120 min after injection using a glucometer (Contour NEXT, Bayer Healthcare). Blood glucose levels were plotted against time, and the area under the curve was calculated.
Adipose tissue stromal vascular cell isolation
Visceral and subcutaneous adipose tissues were finely minced and digested (Adipose tissue dissociation kit, Miltenyi Biotec) using gentleMACS™ Octo Dissociator with Heaters (Miltenyi Biotec). Digested tissues were filtered using a 100-μm cell strainer, washed with DMEM, and centrifuged for 10 min at 300 g/4 °C. After removing the supernatant containing mature adipocytes, the cell pellet was incubated for 2 min in red blood cell lysis buffer (Sigma) at room temperature. The lysis process was neutralized with DMEM media supplemented with 10% FBS. Cells were then centrifuged for 5 min at 300g, and the pellet was dissolved in 10% FBS/DMEM and further processed for in vitro culture or CyTOF analysis.
CyTOF staining and data processing
The dissociated cells were washed in cell staining medium (CSM) and each sample was barcoded prior to staining according to the protocol described in the Cell-ID 20-Plex Pd Barcoding kit (Fluidigm). Barcoded samples were pooled into 1 sample and stained with metal-tagged antibodies as previously described [30]. Fixation and permeabilization were conducted according to the manufacturer’s instructions (Foxp3/Transcription Factor Staining Buffer Set, eBioscience). Finally, samples were washed and suspended in Maxpar Cell Acquisition Solution containing EQ normalization beads (Fluidigm; #201,237) and data was collected by Helios instrument. FCS files were normalized using EQ beads by the Helios software. All data processing and analysis was done with Cytobank Enterprise (Beckman Coulter).
In vitro cell culture
SVF cells from individual mice were pooled within the respective AL and IF-treatment groups. Single cells from each treatment group were harvested and seeded onto 24-well plates in base medium containing high-glucose DMEM (Gibco), supplemented with 10% FBS, penicillin–streptomycin, gentamicin, sodium pyruvate, and MEM non-essential amino acid solution. At confluency, cells were cultured in adipogenic differentiation medium (DMEM with 10% FBS, 0.5 mM IBMX, 1 μM dexamethasone, 1 μM rosiglitazone, and 5 μg/mL insulin) for 2 days. Thereafter, the culture medium was changed to maintenance medium (base medium plus 5 μg/mL insulin) and was changed every 2 days until the end of the study. On day 8 of culture, cells were washed with PBS and either collected for gene expression analysis or fixed with 4% PFA for 30 min, washed in PBS, and then stained with LipidTox (Thermo Fisher) to assess lipid droplet accumulation. Nuclei were stained with DAPI. Final washes were done with PBS and cells were imaged using a Axio Observer (Zeiss) microscope. Zeiss image analysis software was used to calculate lipid droplet area and counts.
RNA isolation and qPCR analysis
Tissues or in vitro cultured cells were lysed using TRIzol (Thermo Fisher). For in vitro samples, each individual sample consisted of pooled RNA from 2 wells. Total RNA was extracted using the RNeasy Mini Kit (Qiagen). Complementary DNA was synthesized using M-MLV reverse transcriptase (Thermo Fisher) and qPCR was performed using the SYBR green methods on QuantStudio 5 (Applied Biosystems). Relative CT values were normalized to the 36B4 gene. Primer sequences are available upon request.
Histological analysis
Upon harvesting, adipose tissues were fixed in 4% PFA, processed and embedded in paraffin, and cut to 5-µm sections. The sections were stained with hematoxylin and eosin for imaging and images were obtained using the Zeiss Axioscan 7. We used CellProfiler (http://cellprofiler.org) to quantify adipocyte size.
Statistical analysis
All data are presented as means ± SEM. Statistical significance of differences between two groups was determined by two-tailed unpaired Student’s t-test. Analysis of covariance (ANCOVA) was performed for energy expenditure data to correct for body weight using CalR. All statistical tests were applied as indicated and p < 0.05 was considered significant.
Intermittent fasting reduces adiposity and improves glucose metabolism in aged mice
To test the impact of IF on WAT phenotypes in aged mice, 75-week-old mice were subjected to a 2:1 IF regimen, comprised of repeated cycles of 2 days of feeding and 1 day of fasting for 10 weeks, as previously described (Fig. 1A) [31]. Mice undergoing IF exhibited a lower body weight compared to ad-libitum (AL)-fed mice (Fig. 1B,C). Body composition analysis showed decreased overall body fat but not lean mass under IF treatment, primarily in the visceral perigonadal fat (pWAT), with no significant differences in the accumulation of subcutaneous inguinal WAT (iWAT), BAT, or liver (Fig. 1D,E). Further, IF treatment resulted in a significant reduction in overall adipocyte size and increased frequency of small adipocytes in pWAT (Fig. 1F,G). To test the effects of IF on glucose metabolism, we performed an intraperitoneal glucose tolerance test (ipGTT) and insulin tolerance test (ipITT) on each mouse. IF decreased age-induced glucose intolerance and improved insulin sensitivity during the GTT and ITT, respectively, as indicated by significantly lower areas under the curve (AUC) (Supplementary Fig. 1A,B). Previous studies have suggested that IF regimens increase energy expenditure (EE) and promote thermogenesis in younger animals [24, 32, 33]. We performed indirect calorimetry analysis on the mice and observed no differences in caloric intake (Supplementary Fig. 1C) but significantly lower EE averaged over a 3-day feeding/fast cycle, assessed by O2 consumption, in IF-treated mice compared to AL mice (Supplementary Fig. 1D,E). These differences remained significant when the analysis of covariates (ANCOVA) was used to correct for total body mass. Together, these data indicate that IF attenuates aging-induced visceral fat mass accumulation as well as glucose intolerance and insulin sensitivity without increasing energy expenditure.
IF induces remodeling of PDGFRα+ ASPCs in aged pWAT
Recent studies have identified transcriptomic differences in the ASPC fraction, a heterogeneous population that includes preadipocytes, adipose stem cells, and adipogenesis-regulatory cells, that are linked with adiposity, metabolic dysfunction, and cellular senescence [13, 15, 34]. We previously demonstrated depot-specific cellular differences in WAT using single-cell proteomic mass cytometry (CyTOF) analysis in young mice on an obesogenic diet and identified adipose progenitor cells as one of the earliest cell types to respond to metabolic challenges [30]. To interrogate the effects of IF on cellular phenotypes at the proteomic level in aged mice, we performed CyTOF analysis on pWAT and iWAT using a panel of 38 metal-tagged antibodies (Table 1). After 2D gating to identify ASPCs (CD45− CD31−) and immune cells, we performed t-Distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding (tSNE), a dimensionality reduction algorithm that enables visualization of high-parameter single-cell data (Fig. 2A) [35]. Analysis of viSNE plots and representative heatmap showed no significant differences in total ASPC number or the expression levels of ASPC markers in PWAT between AL- and IF-treated mice (Supplemental Fig. 2A-D). Next, to minimize gating bias in our analysis of specific ASPC populations, we performed unsupervised clustering of the ASPCs using the FlowSOM algorithm. The FlowSOM analysis separated the cells into 15 distinct metaclusters (Fig. 2B). Four clusters, 8, 9, 10, and 14, expressed Pdgfrα, a marker of adipocyte precursors [36] (Fig. 2C). The proportion of the largest Pdgfrα-expressing precursor fraction, cluster 8, was significantly increased in IF-treated pWAT, compared to pWAT from AL-fed mice (Fig. 2C,D). However, there were no significant differences in the proportion of other ASPC subsets in pWAT (Fig. 2D; Supplementary Fig. 2E). Our CyTOF panel included markers such as DPP4, ICAM1, CD142, CD9, and Ly6c that have been reported to be indicative of stem-like activity, adipogenesis, and inflammatory activity in adipocyte progenitor populations [13, 37]. We compared surface expression levels of these markers in each of the identified ASPC clusters; however, their median expression was not altered by IF treatment in aged pWAT in our study (Supplementary Fig. 2C-F). We performed similar tSNE and FlowSOM cluster analysis of ASPCs in iWAT. No significant differences were observed in any of the Pdgfrα+ or Pdgfrα− FlowSOM clusters in iWAT of IF-treated and AL-treated mice (Supplementary Fig. 2G,H). Collectively, these data indicate that the IF intervention resulted in targeted remodeling of a distinct subset of ASPCs in aged pWAT.

IF induces remodeling of eosinophils in aged pWAT
Few studies have examined aging-induced changes to the immune landscape of adipose tissues at the proteomic level. We assessed the impact of age on immune cell populations by comparing proportions of immune cells in pWAT from young 3-month-old and aged 20-month-old mice using CyTOF analysis. While there were no differences in the overall frequency of immune cells among live singlets in young and old mice (Fig. 3A), there were marked differences in the proportion of key myeloid and lymphoid cell populations. Figure 3B depicts CD45-expressing immune populations in young and aged mice, where the extended tabs in the donut-shaped graphs represent populations that are proportionally significantly different between the 2 groups. Among the myeloid fraction of cells in pWAT, the proportion of dendritic cells (DC-1 and DC-2, CD11b+ and CD11b−, respectively) and eosinophils were significantly reduced in aged pWAT compared to young mice, but there were no differences in monocytes or in any of the macrophage populations (Fig. 3B,C). Adaptive immune cells, namely, B cells and T cell subsets, were significantly increased in aged pWAT, whereas NK cells and ILC2 were markedly reduced compared to young animals (Fig. 3B,C).
We next characterized IF-induced remodeling of the immune cell landscape in adipose tissue of aged mice. tSNE analysis and unsupervised FlowSOM clustering of the CD45+ hematopoietic cells revealed that although the IF intervention did not alter the overall proportion of immune cells (Supplementary Fig. 3A), the proportion of eosinophils, shown as cluster17 on the FlowSOM map, was markedly elevated in IF-treated vs AL-treated pWAT (Fig. 3D–F). IF did not result in remodeling of any other lymphoid or myeloid cell populations in pWAT of aged mice (Fig. 3G,H), identified based on surface marker expression patterns (Supplementary Fig. 3B-E). Additionally, the IF regimen did not significantly impact eosinophil frequency or any other immune populations in iWAT (Supplementary Fig. 3F-H). These data suggest that eosinophils may be a cellular target of IF-mediated remodeling of the pWAT adipose immune compartment of aged mice. We performed gene expression analysis of total adipose tissue to determine whether the observed cellular changes resulted in inflammatory or adipogenic modulation at the whole tissue level. Leptin levels were significantly decreased under IF treatment (Fig. 3I), which is known to be associated with reduction in adiposity and adipose inflammation [38, 39]. However, while a reduction in adiposity was observed (Fig. 1D–G), there were no significant differences in expression of genes related to adipogenesis, senescence, or fibro-inflammation in pWAT or iWAT of IF-treated vs AL-fed groups (Fig. 3I; Supplementary Fig. 3I).
IF reduces senescent phenotypes and rejuvenates adipogenic potential of pWAT ASPCs
Our CyTOF data revealed significant remodeling of the aged Pdgfrα+ ASPCs in pWAT after IF. To assess the functional implications of this remodeling, we harvested SVF cells from pWAT and induced adipogenic differentiation in vitro following the schematic diagram shown in Fig. 4A. ASPCs from IF-treated mice exhibited improved adipogenic capacity resulting in increased differentiation to mature adipocytes, increased lipid droplet accumulation (Fig. 4B,C), and markedly increased expression of adipogenic genes including adiponectin (Adipoq) and PPARγ2, compared to ASPCs derived from AL-fed mice (Fig. 4D). Differentiated ASPCs from IF-treated mice displayed significantly lower expression of senescent markers p16INK4 and p21, indicating a reduction in senescent phenotypes. Overall, these findings suggest that IF can restore the capacity of ASPCs for hyperplastic growth and de novo differentiation, concomitant with reduced senescence and indicate a potential role of pWAT ASPCs as early responders to metabolic and energy changes in adipose tissue.
Although caloric restriction and IF are reported to elicit multiple health benefits in younger populations, their safety and efficacy in older populations is not well addressed. In particular, aging is already associated with increased metabolic stressors and frailty due to loss of lean mass, making it crucial to understand the implications of these dietary interventions in aged populations. We show in the current study that a 10-week IF intervention in 18–20-month-old mice, which corresponds to approximately 60 years of age in humans, leads to reduced fat accumulation, and induces cellular remodeling in visceral fat. Notably, this intervention preserved lean mass. A hallmark of aging in both humans and mice is a decline in lean mass, quality, and strength [40]. Our finding raises the potential for the IF regimen to be considered as a safe regimen for improving health in obese and/or overweight human populations, which should be addressed in clinical studies. Previous studies in younger mice have reported that IF and time-restricted feeding regimens reduced body weight and fat mass through increased energy expenditure and thermogenesis [33, 41]. Aged mice, however, have been shown to exhibit blunted thermogenic capacity [42], which likely explains the lack of increased energy expenditure observed in the present study in the mice on the IF regimen. Our findings suggest that the cellular mechanisms that mediate metabolic improvements with IF differ in aged and young populations. WAT is one of the first organs to exhibit dysregulatory responses to aging [9, 10, 43], and previous studies have shown that increased expression of senescent markers in adipose progenitor cells in obese older adults is associated with decreased proliferative capacity, reduced expression of adipogenic markers, and increased BMI [2]. Our IF intervention induced functional cellular remodeling of the pWAT ASPCs, resulting in reduced senescent APCs, renewed capacity for adipogenesis, and hyperplastic growth and reduced pWAT mass and hypertrophy. Intriguingly, IF increased the proportion of a DPP4-negative ASPC population (Cluster 8), which is known to have higher adipogenic capacity than the DPP4-expressing self-renewing cell population [44, 45].
